mj-text-bKWuFGBO.content:
Hi {{contact.first_name}},

Here's what everyone got wrong about multifamily in 2026:

They're all looking at the same five markets.

While the real opportunity is somewhere else entirely.

Multifamily starts are down 53% from 2022 highs. Headline markets (Miami, Austin, Phoenix) are working through oversupply. New supply has slowed dramatically across the board.

But here's the pattern nobody's talking about:

The best returns aren't coming from the markets with the most capital. They're coming from the markets that were overlooked while everyone chased the hype.

South Bay (CA). Ventura County. Hillsborough (OR). Lake Oswego. Louisville South Central.

These aren't sexy. They won't get you invited to panels. But they have something better: strong fundamentals nobody's competing for yet.

This month:

✅ The next multifamily opportunity isn't where everyone's looking
✅ Why the housing market isn't stalled—it's resetting
✅ June 2026 rate update + what's actually happening

Let's dig in.

mj-text-bKWuFGBO-piT1bAyZ-XkxBz27A.content: → Why Overlooked Submarkets with Strong Fundamentals Are Attracting Investor Attention as New Development Slows

mj-text-zUU7TNhr.content:
Zoning constraints in certain submarkets mean zero new supply. Weak demand in others means supply shouldn't exist. The difference is everything.

Here's what institutional capital is doing right now:

They're not competing in Miami, Austin, Phoenix (headline markets flooded with new supply).

They're buying in South Bay (CA). Ventura County. Hillsborough (OR). Lake Oswego. Louisville South Central.

Why? Supply constraints + high occupancy (94%+) + durable demand = pricing power.

Compare:

  • Miami: High new supply, 1.2% rent growth, extreme competition

  • South Bay: Zoning limits supply, 2.3% rent growth, minimal competition

What makes a submarket a winner:

✅ Strong employment bases
✅ High occupancy (94%+)
✅ Limited new supply from 2020-2024
✅ Supply constraints have a reason (zoning/geography, not weak demand)

Avoid:

❌ New supply in pipeline despite low occupancy
❌ Zoning changes coming that unlock development
❌ Falling occupancy + new supply arriving

The takeaway:

The next multifamily winners aren't in the markets getting all the capital. They're in the supply-constrained submarkets nobody's competing for yet.

mj-text-iwAeSLUN.content: 🏠 The Housing Market Isn't Stalled—It's Resetting

mj-text-TwppHz2a.content: → Experts expected lower rates and stronger sales entering 2026.

mj-text-C6AJmHxe.content:
Both forecasts were revised DOWN.

Here's what actually happened:

  • Rates stayed elevated (inflation + geopolitical risk)

  • Existing home sales slowed

  • New construction sales slowed

  • But: Home prices still projected to rise nationally

  • And: Pent-up demand still exists

Translation: Buyers are waiting for perfect. Perfect may not come.

The real opportunity:

Most buyers are on the sidelines. That means:

  • Lower competition

  • Higher negotiating power

  • Builder incentives still available

  • Rate buydowns still negotiable

If you find a home that works at today's rates and you'll stay 5+ years? This is actually the time to move.

The buyers waiting for 5% rates in 2027 will regret it when everyone rushes back and competition spikes.

mj-text-DKUspLNS.content: 💰June 2026 Interest Rate Snapshot

mj-text-dcG0v2OP.content:
Here’s where financing stands this month for both multifamily and single-family deals.

Sources: Residential rates sourced from Freddie Mac, Fortune, Bankrate, U.S. News, Zillow as of June 4-10, 2026. Commercial rates sourced from Commercial Loan Direct, Apartment Loan Store, Select Commercial as of June 3-8, 2026. Market Context: Rates stabilizing in mid-6% range. Iran conflict continues pushing oil prices and inflation higher. Job market strong, inflation sticky at 3.8%+. Fed on pause, no cuts expected near-term. Housing affordability marginally improving despite elevated rates.

mj-text-Lvtk2ipD.content: 🔑 Key Takeaway: Market Wisdom for June 2026

mj-text-sr3BwGDZ.content:
If you're a multifamily investor:

Stop evaluating metros. Start evaluating submarkets.

Miami is full. Austin is full. Phoenix is full.

But South Bay? Ventura? Hillsborough? Lake Oswego?

High occupancy. Limited supply. Durable demand. Minimal competition.

That's where capital should be flowing. Not where it already is.

If you're a single-family buyer:

The market isn't waiting for you. You need to decide: Wait for perfect rates, or move now while you have leverage?

Forecasts show rates staying elevated through 2026. Home prices still rising. Pent-up demand still building.

If you find a home that works, today's market is giving you something rare: negotiating power.

mj-text-eY3Yqusd.content: Ready to Stop Waiting and Start Building?

mj-text-3rQjpfCg.content:
July will bring more investors to South Bay, Ventura, Lake Oswego once they figure it out.

August will bring higher cap rate compression in those markets.

September will bring tighter terms and faster competition.

June still has the signal. The clarity. The positioning window.

Most investors follow the crowd. You're not most investors. You're reading this while the data still shows the opportunity before everyone else notices.

Use it.

We're working with investors right now on submarket evaluation frameworks.

Not chasing headlines. Identifying supply constraints before they price in.

Brian Norton
Principal, Olive Tree Investments
